title: Welcome to Unity: Hub, Editor & Your First Roll-A-Ball
html:
This is your engine

You finished Foundations and you chose Unity as your single path. Good choice. Unity is a battle-tested, C#-driven engine behind countless shipped games across every genre, and from here on this track stands entirely on its own. You will not need any other engine to go from zero to a real portfolio.

Here is the portfolio you will build across these nine lessons, each in this one engine:

  • Roll-A-Ball — a 3D arcade collect-em-up (starts today).
  • 2D Platformer — jumping, gravity, tiles.
  • Top-down Twin-Stick Shooter — aim and fire in all directions.
  • Endless Runner — procedural spawning and scoring.
  • Brick-Breaker — physics-driven arcade.
  • Tower Defense — prefabs, waves, and enemy paths.
  • Puzzle game — grid logic and state.
  • Capstone — one game polished into a portfolio-ready build.

Seven genres, one engine, your name on all of them.

Unity Hub: your launcher and project manager

Unity ships in two pieces. Unity Hub is a desktop app that manages engine versions and projects. The Unity Editor is the actual game-making tool. You install one or more Editor versions through the Hub, then create projects against them.

  1. Download Unity Hub from unity.com and sign in with a (free) Unity account.
  2. In the Hub, open the Installs tab and add a recent LTS (Long-Term Support) Editor version. LTS releases are the stable ones you want for learning and shipping.
  3. During install, include the build support modules for your platform (Windows/Mac) plus WebGL so you can later publish playable browser builds.
Tip.
Always pick an LTS version. Bleeding-edge releases break tutorials and plugins. Stability beats novelty while you learn.

Creating a project

In the Hub's Projects tab, click New project. You choose a template (3D, 2D, URP, etc.), a name, and a folder. Templates just preconfigure render settings and packages — pick the plain 3D template for today.

The Editor layout

Unity's Editor is a set of dockable panels. The five you live in:

PanelWhat it does
Scene3D/2D viewport where you arrange your world.
GameWhat the player's camera actually sees when you press Play.
HierarchyA list of every object in the current scene.
InspectorProperties of whatever is selected.
ProjectAll files (assets) on disk for this project.

The toolbar at top center holds Play, Pause, and Step. Press Play to run your game inside the Editor; press it again to stop.

Danger.
Changes you make to objects while Play is running are discarded when you stop. The Editor tints slightly to remind you. Never tune values in Play mode and expect them to stick.

Navigating the Scene view

Right-drag to look around. Hold right-drag and use W A S D to fly (flythrough mode). Scroll to zoom, and press F with an object selected to frame it. The colored gizmo at top-right snaps to axis views. Master this now — you will spend hundreds of hours in this viewport.

Lab.
Create a 3D project. Add a Cube via GameObject > 3D Object > Cube. Practice flying around it, framing it with F, and switching to top/front views with the gizmo. Press Play and confirm nothing crashes. Save the scene with Ctrl/Cmd+S.
🎮 Build This: 3D Arcade — Roll-A-Ball (Part 1)

This STARTS a new genre: a 3D arcade collect-em-up, the foundation game we extend over the next lessons.

  1. Create a new 3D project named RollABall.
  2. Add a Plane (GameObject > 3D Object > Plane) as the floor and rename it Ground.
  3. Add a Sphere, rename it Player, and move it just above the plane.
  4. In the Inspector, set the Player's Position to (0, 0.5, 0) so it rests on the ground.
  5. Press Play and confirm you can see the sphere on the plane in the Game view.
  6. Save the scene as Main inside an Assets/Scenes folder.

title: GameObjects, Components & the Inspector
html:
Everything is a GameObject

A Unity scene is a tree of GameObjects. A GameObject by itself does almost nothing — it is an empty container with a name and a position. Its behavior comes entirely from the components attached to it. This is the single most important mental model in Unity: GameObject = container, Component = behavior.

That Sphere you made? It is a GameObject carrying a Transform, a Mesh Filter (the shape), a Mesh Renderer (how it draws), and a Sphere Collider (its physical bounds). Strip those components and you have a nameless empty point in space.

The Transform: the one component every object has

Every GameObject has exactly one Transform, and you cannot remove it. It stores three things:

  • Position — where it is, in world or local units.
  • Rotation — its orientation, shown in degrees (Euler angles).
  • Scale — its size multiplier per axis.

Unity uses a left-handed, Y-up coordinate system: X is right, Y is up, Z is forward. Get comfortable reading the gizmo colors — red=X, green=Y, blue=Z.

Parenting and the Hierarchy

Drag one object onto another in the Hierarchy to make it a child. A child's Transform becomes relative to its parent: move the parent and children follow; the child's Inspector now shows local position. This is how you build composite objects — a car body with wheel children, a player with a camera child.

Tip.
Use empty GameObjects (GameObject > Create Empty) as organizational folders in the Hierarchy. Name one ---Environment--- and parent your ground, walls, and props under it to keep large scenes navigable.

Working in the Inspector

Select any object and the Inspector shows a stacked list of its components, each with editable fields. You can:

  • Add Component at the bottom to attach new behavior (a Rigidbody, a script, a Light).
  • Toggle a component's checkbox to enable/disable it without removing it.
  • Right-click a component header to Reset, Remove, or Copy/Paste its values.

Transform tools

The top-left toolbar gives you handles in the Scene view, bound to hotkeys:

KeyTool
QPan (Hand)
WMove
ERotate
RScale

Materials: giving objects color

A bare object renders matte gray. To color it you create a Material asset (right-click in Project > Create > Material), set its Albedo (base color), and drag the material onto the object. Materials are reusable assets — assign the same one to many objects and changing it updates them all.

Lab.
Select your Ground plane and create a green Material and drag it on. Create a second Material in blue and apply it to the Player sphere. Create an empty GameObject named Walls and parent four scaled cubes under it to box in the play area.
🎮 Build This: 3D Arcade — Roll-A-Ball (Part 2)

This EXTENDS your Roll-A-Ball from Lesson 1, dressing the play area.

  1. Give the Ground a green material and the Player a distinct blue material.
  2. Create an empty GameObject named Walls at the world origin.
  3. Add a Cube, scale it long and thin (e.g. (0.5, 2, 20)), position it at one edge of the plane, and parent it under Walls.
  4. Duplicate it with Ctrl/Cmd+D three times and arrange the copies to enclose all four sides of the ground.
  5. Add an empty GameObject named Pickups; you will fill it later. Save the scene.

title: C# Scripting with MonoBehaviour
html:
Scripts are components you write

You have used built-in components. Now you write your own. A Unity script is a C# class that inherits from MonoBehaviour. Once attached to a GameObject it is a component, appearing in the Inspector like any other.

Create one via right-click in Project > Create > C# Script. Name it carefully — the file name must match the class name. Double-click to open it in your code editor (VS Code, Rider, or Visual Studio).

The MonoBehaviour lifecycle

Unity calls special methods on your script at specific times. You do not call these yourself — the engine does.

MethodWhen it runs
Awake()Once, when the object loads — before anything else.
Start()Once, just before the first frame.
Update()Every frame. Game logic and input go here.
FixedUpdate()On a fixed physics timestep. Physics code goes here.

A first script

Spinner.cs
// Rotates whatever it is attached to. using UnityEngine; public class Spinner : MonoBehaviour { // public fields appear in the Inspector public float speed = 90f; void Update() { // Time.deltaTime makes motion frame-rate independent transform.Rotate(Vector3.up * speed * Time.deltaTime); } }

Frame-rate independence

Update() runs more often on fast machines. If you moved an object by a fixed amount each frame, it would move faster on a gaming PC than a laptop. The fix is Time.deltaTime — the seconds elapsed since the last frame. Multiply movement by it and your speed becomes units per second, identical on every machine.

Public fields and the Inspector

Any public field shows up in the Inspector and can be tuned without recompiling. This is huge: you can balance the game by dragging sliders. To expose a private field too, add [SerializeField] above it — preferred, because it keeps the field private to other code while still editable.

Tip.
Prefer [SerializeField] private float speed; over public float speed;. You get Inspector editing without letting unrelated scripts mutate your value. Encapsulation matters even in game code.

Reading player input early

To read movement, the classic approach uses Input.GetAxis("Horizontal") and "Vertical", which return a value from -1 to 1 from WASD/arrow keys. We will upgrade to the modern Input System in Lesson 6, but this older API is perfect for getting your ball rolling today.

Lab.
Write the Spinner script above, attach it to a Cube, press Play, and watch it spin. Then change speed in the Inspector during Play to see live tuning (remember the value resets on stop).
🎮 Build This: 3D Arcade — Roll-A-Ball (Part 3)

This EXTENDS your Roll-A-Ball: the ball now responds to keys.

  1. Create a script PlayerController.cs and attach it to the Player sphere.
  2. In Update(), read float h = Input.GetAxis("Horizontal"); and float v = Input.GetAxis("Vertical");.
  3. Add a [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 8f; field.
  4. For now, move directly: transform.Translate(new Vector3(h, 0, v) * moveSpeed * Time.deltaTime);.
  5. Press Play and steer the ball with WASD. Notice it slides through walls — we fix that with physics next lesson.

title: Physics: Rigidbody, Colliders & Triggers
html:
Letting the engine push things for you

So far you moved objects by editing their Transform directly. That ignores physics — your ball slid through walls. To get collisions, gravity, and momentum, you hand control to Unity's physics engine by adding a Rigidbody.

Rigidbody: the physics body

Add a Rigidbody (3D) or Rigidbody2D to a GameObject and the engine now drives its motion. Gravity pulls it down, forces push it, and it stops at colliders. Key fields:

  • Mass — heavier bodies resist forces more.
  • Drag — air resistance that slows linear motion.
  • Use Gravity — toggle the downward pull.
  • Is Kinematic — if on, the body ignores forces and you move it by script; used for platforms and scripted motion.
  • Constraints — freeze position or rotation on chosen axes.
Danger.
Once an object has a Rigidbody, do not move it with transform.position. That teleports it past the physics solver and causes tunneling and jitter. Move it with rb.MovePosition() or by applying forces inside FixedUpdate().

Colliders: the shape physics sees

A Collider defines the volume that participates in collisions. Primitives (Box, Sphere, Capsule) are cheap and accurate; Mesh Collider matches arbitrary geometry but costs more. A collider does not require a Rigidbody — static level geometry (walls, floors) uses colliders with no Rigidbody, and the physics engine treats them as immovable.

Forces vs. velocity

Inside FixedUpdate() you influence a Rigidbody two main ways:

PlayerController.cs
using UnityEngine; public class PlayerController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float moveForce = 20f; private Rigidbody rb; private float h, v; void Awake() { rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody>(); } void Update() { h = Input.GetAxis("Horizontal"); v = Input.GetAxis("Vertical"); } void FixedUpdate() { // push the ball; physics handles the wall stops rb.AddForce(new Vector3(h, 0, v) * moveForce); } }

Note the split: read input in Update() (runs every frame, catches every keypress) but apply force in FixedUpdate() (runs on the physics clock).

Triggers: overlap without collision

Sometimes you want to detect overlap without a physical bounce — a coin pickup, a checkpoint, a damage zone. Check a collider's Is Trigger box and it stops blocking movement but still fires callbacks:

CallbackFires when
OnCollisionEnterTwo solid colliders hit.
OnTriggerEnterSomething enters a trigger volume.
OnTriggerExitSomething leaves a trigger volume.

For a trigger to fire, at least one of the two objects must have a Rigidbody. Tags (set at the top of the Inspector) let you check what entered: if (other.CompareTag("Pickup")).

Lab.
Add a Rigidbody to your Player sphere and rewrite movement to use AddForce in FixedUpdate. Confirm the ball now bumps off the walls instead of passing through.
🎮 Build This: 3D Arcade — Roll-A-Ball (Part 4: collectibles)

This EXTENDS your Roll-A-Ball into a real collect-em-up.

  1. Give the Player a Rigidbody and switch movement to AddForce in FixedUpdate.
  2. Add a small Cube as a pickup. Create a tag Pickup and assign it. Check its collider's Is Trigger, and give it a glowing material plus a Spinner script for flair.
  3. On the Player, add OnTriggerEnter(Collider other); if other.CompareTag("Pickup"), call Destroy(other.gameObject) and increment an int score.
  4. Duplicate the pickup a dozen times around the arena, parented under Pickups.
  5. Play and roll around collecting cubes. Log the score with Debug.Log(score).

title: Going 2D: Sprites, Prefabs & a Platformer
html:
A whole new genre: 2D

Unity is just as strong in 2D as 3D. Internally a 2D game is a 3D scene viewed straight down the Z axis with an orthographic camera (no perspective). The physics swaps to a 2D variant: Rigidbody2D, BoxCollider2D, CircleCollider2D. Time to start a brand-new genre — a side-scrolling platformer.

Sprites and the SpriteRenderer

A Sprite is a 2D image asset. Import a PNG, set its Texture Type to Sprite (2D and UI) in the Inspector, and drag it into the scene — Unity wraps it in a GameObject with a SpriteRenderer. The Pixels Per Unit setting controls how many texture pixels equal one world unit; keep it consistent across your art so everything scales together.

Sorting: who draws in front

In 2D, draw order is controlled by Sorting Layers and Order in Layer on the SpriteRenderer, not by depth. Create sorting layers like Background, Default, Player, Foreground so your character always renders over the scenery.

Prefabs: reusable GameObject blueprints

Here is one of Unity's most powerful features. A Prefab is a saved GameObject — components, children, values, and all — stored as an asset you can stamp out repeatedly. Make one coin, save it as a prefab, and every coin in your level is an instance of that prefab.

The magic is propagation: edit the prefab asset and every instance updates instantly. Build a hundred platforms, then decide they need a different collider — change the prefab once.

  1. Drag a configured GameObject from the Hierarchy into the Project window to create a prefab.
  2. Drag the prefab back into the scene to make instances.
  3. Double-click the prefab to enter Prefab Mode and edit the master copy.
  4. Overrides on an instance (bolded fields) can be applied back to the prefab or reverted.
Tip.
Spawn prefabs at runtime with Instantiate(prefab, position, rotation). You will lean on this for bullets, enemies, and obstacles in the next several genres. Drag the prefab into a [SerializeField] private GameObject field to reference it.

A 2D move + jump script

PlatformerMove.cs
using UnityEngine; public class PlatformerMove : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float speed = 6f; [SerializeField] private float jump = 12f; [SerializeField] private Transform groundCheck; [SerializeField] private LayerMask groundMask; private Rigidbody2D rb; void Awake() { rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); } void Update() { float x = Input.GetAxis("Horizontal"); rb.velocity = new Vector2(x * speed, rb.velocity.y); bool grounded = Physics2D.OverlapCircle(groundCheck.position, 0.1f, groundMask); if (grounded && Input.GetButtonDown("Jump")) rb.velocity = new Vector2(rb.velocity.x, jump); } }

The ground check is the standard trick: an empty child at the character's feet, plus a tiny overlap test against a Ground layer, so you can only jump when standing on something.

Lab.
Create a 2D scene. Make a player sprite with Rigidbody2D + BoxCollider2D, a few ground sprites with colliders on a Ground layer, and an empty GroundCheck child at the feet. Wire up the script and confirm you can run and jump.
🎮 Build This: 2D Platformer — Hop Quest (new genre)

This STARTS a new genre: a 2D side-scrolling platformer (separate from Roll-A-Ball).

  1. New 2D scene with an orthographic camera, a player sprite (Rigidbody2D + collider + GroundCheck), and the PlatformerMove script.
  2. Build a few platforms; turn one into a Prefab and stamp out a level.
  3. Make a Coin prefab: sprite, CircleCollider2D set to Is Trigger, spinning or bobbing for life.
  4. On the player, OnTriggerEnter2D destroys coins and adds to a score.
  5. Add a Goal trigger that logs "You win!" when reached. Play, run, jump, collect, finish.

title: The Input System & a Twin-Stick Shooter
html:
Upgrading how you read input

The old Input.GetAxis API is simple but rigid — it hard-codes keys and does not handle rebinding or gamepads cleanly. Modern Unity ships the Input System package: an action-based, device-agnostic system that supports keyboard, mouse, and controllers at once, with rebinding for free. A twin-stick shooter — move with one stick, aim/fire with the other — is the perfect genre to learn it.

Installing and the action map

  1. Open Window > Package Manager, find Input System, and install it. Accept the prompt to enable the new backend (the Editor restarts).
  2. Create an Input Actions asset (Create > Input Actions) and double-click it.
  3. Define an Action Map (e.g. Gameplay) containing Actions: Move (Value, Vector2), Aim (Value, Vector2), Fire (Button).
  4. Add Bindings: WASD and the left stick for Move, mouse position / right stick for Aim, left-click / right-trigger for Fire.
  5. Tick Generate C# Class so you get a typed wrapper to reference in code.
Tip.
Think in actions, not keys. "Fire" is an intent; whether it comes from a mouse button or a trigger is a binding detail. This separation is why rebinding and controller support become trivial.

Reading actions in code

The simplest path adds a PlayerInput component to your player, points it at the actions asset, and lets it call methods. Or read directly from the generated class:

ShipController.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.InputSystem; public class ShipController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float speed = 8f; [SerializeField] private GameObject bulletPrefab; [SerializeField] private Transform muzzle; private Rigidbody2D rb; private Vector2 moveInput; void Awake() { rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); } // called by PlayerInput "Move" action public void OnMove(InputAction.CallbackContext ctx) { moveInput = ctx.ReadValue<Vector2>(); } public void OnFire(InputAction.CallbackContext ctx) { if (ctx.performed) Instantiate(bulletPrefab, muzzle.position, transform.rotation); } void Update() { // aim toward the mouse in world space Vector3 m = Camera.main.ScreenToWorldPoint(Mouse.current.position.ReadValue()); Vector2 dir = ((Vector2)m - rb.position).normalized; transform.up = dir; } void FixedUpdate() { rb.velocity = moveInput * speed; } }

Aiming and firing

In a top-down shooter the ship rotates to face the aim direction (transform.up = dir in 2D), and firing spawns a bullet prefab at a muzzle point. The bullet carries its own script: a Rigidbody2D given forward velocity in Start(), plus a self-Destroy after a few seconds so bullets do not pile up forever.

Danger.
Always destroy spawned bullets and enemies — by lifetime or off-screen check. A shooter that Instantiates without ever destroying will leak thousands of objects and tank your frame rate within seconds.
Lab.
Install the Input System, build a Gameplay action map with Move/Aim/Fire, add a PlayerInput component to your ship, and confirm the ship moves with WASD and rotates to face the mouse.
🎮 Build This: Twin-Stick Shooter — Star Sweeper (new genre)

This STARTS a new genre: a top-down twin-stick shooter, using the Input System and prefab spawning you just learned.

  1. 2D top-down scene; a ship sprite with Rigidbody2D (gravity scale 0) and the ShipController script.
  2. Make a Bullet prefab: sprite, Rigidbody2D, a script that moves it forward and self-destructs after 2s; trigger collider tagged Bullet.
  3. Make an Enemy prefab that drifts toward the player; on bullet trigger it dies and adds score.
  4. Add an EnemySpawner script that Instantiates enemies at the screen edges on a timer.
  5. Move with WASD/left stick, aim with mouse/right stick, fire to clear waves. Track score.

title: UI with Canvas/uGUI, an Endless Runner & a Brick-Breaker
html:
Putting things on screen

Every score counter, health bar, menu, and button lives on a Canvas — Unity's UI layer (uGUI). UI elements are GameObjects too, but they render in screen space and use a special RectTransform instead of a plain Transform. This lesson pairs UI with two new genres: a fast endless runner (reusing input and spawning) and a physics-driven brick-breaker.

The Canvas and render modes

Add GameObject > UI > Canvas and Unity creates a Canvas plus an EventSystem (which handles clicks). The Canvas has a Render Mode:

ModeUse
Screen Space - OverlayDrawn flat over everything. Default for HUDs and menus.
Screen Space - CameraRendered by a camera; allows perspective/effects.
World SpaceUI lives in the 3D world (floating health bars, in-world signs).

RectTransform and anchors

The hardest UI concept: anchors. A RectTransform is positioned relative to its parent via anchor points. Anchor a score label to the top-left and it stays top-left on any screen size; anchor a button's edges to stretch and it grows with the window. Master the anchor presets (the little grid widget in the Inspector) and responsive UI becomes easy.

Tip.
Add a Canvas Scaler set to Scale With Screen Size with a reference resolution (e.g. 1920x1080). This keeps your UI proportional across phones, laptops, and monitors instead of tiny on 4K and huge on small screens.

Core UI elements

  • Text - TextMeshPro — crisp text; the modern default. Unity prompts to import TMP Essentials the first time.
  • Image — panels, icons, bars (use the Filled image type for health/progress bars).
  • Button — clickable; wire its OnClick event in the Inspector to call a public method.

Updating UI from code

HUD.cs
using UnityEngine; using TMPro; public class HUD : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private TMP_Text scoreLabel; private int score; public void AddScore(int amount) { score += amount; scoreLabel.text = $"Score: {score}"; } }

Drag the TextMeshPro object into the scoreLabel slot in the Inspector, then call AddScore from gameplay code. Wiring references in the Inspector — instead of finding objects by name in code — is the clean, fast Unity way.

Lab.
Add a Canvas with a Canvas Scaler, a TextMeshPro score label anchored top-left, and a TextMeshPro "Game Over" panel anchored center that starts disabled. Write a small script that toggles the panel on a key press.
🎮 Build This: Endless Runner — Dash Lane (new genre)

This STARTS a new genre: an endless runner. It reuses the Input System (Lesson 6) and prefab spawning (Lesson 5), now driving a live UI HUD.

  1. A player that auto-runs forward; input only switches lanes (left/right) or jumps.
  2. An ObstacleSpawner that Instantiates obstacle prefabs ahead on a timer, scrolling them toward the player; destroy them once passed.
  3. A Canvas HUD with a TextMeshPro distance/score label that ticks up over time via the HUD script.
  4. Colliding with an obstacle ends the run: show a center-anchored Game Over panel with a Restart button whose OnClick reloads the scene.
  5. Speed up over time so it gets harder. Play and chase a high score.
🎮 Build This: Brick-Breaker — Brick Buster (new genre, physics)

This STARTS a new genre: a physics arcade brick-breaker, combining Lesson 4 physics with this lesson's UI.

  1. A paddle with Rigidbody2D (Kinematic) moved left/right by input along the bottom.
  2. A ball with Rigidbody2D and a bouncy Physics Material 2D (friction 0, bounciness 1) so it ricochets off paddle and walls.
  3. Make a Brick prefab with a collider; on collision it is destroyed and adds score via the HUD script. Lay out a grid of bricks.
  4. Bottom edge is a trigger: if the ball falls through, lose a life. Show lives and score on the Canvas.
  5. Clear all bricks to win; show a Win panel. Play and break every brick.

title: Animation, the Animator & a Tower Defense
html:
Bringing things to life

Static games feel dead. Unity's animation system has two halves: the Animation window, where you author clips by keyframing properties over time, and the Animator, a state machine that decides which clip plays and blends between them. We pair this with a new genre built on everything so far — a tower defense, heavy on prefabs and timed waves.

Animation clips

An Animation Clip records property values (position, rotation, scale, color, sprite frame...) over a timeline. Open Window > Animation > Animation, select an object, create a clip, and add keyframes at different times. Unity interpolates between them. This works for anything — a bobbing coin, a pulsing button, a sprite-frame walk cycle, a turret recoil.

The Animator Controller and states

An Animator Controller asset is a graph of states (each playing a clip) connected by transitions. The Animator component on a GameObject runs this graph. You drive transitions with Parameters:

ParameterUse
BoolOn/off states (isWalking).
TriggerOne-shot events (Attack, Die).
FloatContinuous values (Speed) for blend logic.
IntEnumerated states.

You set these from code, and the state machine reacts:

EnemyAnim.cs
using UnityEngine; public class EnemyAnim : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Animator anim; public void SetMoving(bool moving) { anim.SetBool("isWalking", moving); } public void Die() { anim.SetTrigger("Die"); } }
Tip.
Use a Trigger for events that happen once (an explosion, a death) and a Bool for ongoing states (walking, idle). Mixing them up is the most common Animator bug — a Bool that never resets gets stuck.

Waves and spawning logic

A tower defense is a spawning problem dressed as a game. A WaveManager uses coroutines — methods that pause and resume across frames — to spew enemies on a schedule:

WaveManager.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; public class WaveManager : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private GameObject enemyPrefab; [SerializeField] private Transform spawnPoint; IEnumerator SpawnWave(int count) { for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) { Instantiate(enemyPrefab, spawnPoint.position, Quaternion.identity); yield return new WaitForSeconds(0.8f); } } void Start() { StartCoroutine(SpawnWave(10)); } }

Enemies follow a path of waypoints (an array of Transforms, advancing to the next when close). Towers detect enemies in range with Physics.OverlapSphere and fire on a cooldown.

Lab.
Make a coin or turret with an Animator: a bobbing idle clip and a one-shot action clip driven by a Trigger. Fire the trigger from a key press and confirm the state machine transitions and returns to idle.
🎮 Build This: Tower Defense — Last Stand (new genre)

This STARTS a new genre: tower defense, combining prefabs (L5), spawning (L6/L7), UI (L7), and animation.

  1. Lay out a path as a list of waypoint Transforms from a spawn point to a base.
  2. Make an Enemy prefab that walks the waypoints with a walk animation, has health, and damages the base if it arrives.
  3. Build a WaveManager coroutine that spawns increasing waves with delays between them.
  4. Make a Tower prefab that detects enemies in range, fires bullet prefabs on a cooldown, and plays a fire animation.
  5. Add UI for base health and current wave; let the player place towers by clicking buildable spots. Survive all waves to win.

title: Scenes, Builds & the Portfolio Capstone
html:
From project to shippable game

You have built six genres. This final lesson teaches the last pieces — multi-scene flow, persistence, build settings — and then a capstone: a small puzzle game polished into a portfolio-ready, playable build with a real menu. By the end you have a finished thing to show, not just exercises.

Scenes and scene flow

A Scene is a saved arrangement of GameObjects — a level, a menu, a screen. Real games use several: a Main Menu scene, a Gameplay scene, maybe a Win/Lose scene. You switch between them in code:

SceneNav.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.SceneManagement; public class SceneNav : MonoBehaviour { public void LoadGame() { SceneManager.LoadScene("Gameplay"); } public void Restart() { SceneManager.LoadScene(SceneManager.GetActiveScene().name); } public void Quit() { Application.Quit(); } }

Wire these to menu Buttons via their OnClick event. To carry data between scenes (a high score, chosen level), use PlayerPrefs for tiny persistent values, or a DontDestroyOnLoad manager object that survives scene loads.

Build Settings

Open File > Build Settings (or Build Profiles in newer versions). Two essentials:

  1. Scenes In Build — drag every scene you want shipped into the list. Scene index 0 is what launches first. A scene not in this list cannot be loaded in a build, even if it loads fine in the Editor.
  2. Platform — choose Windows, Mac, or WebGL (great for portfolios — it runs in a browser, no install). Click Build and Unity produces a folder you can zip, upload to itch.io, or host.
Danger.
The number-one build-day bug: "my menu's Play button does nothing in the build." Almost always the Gameplay scene was never added to Scenes In Build. Add every scene, and test the actual build — not just Play mode — before you call it done.
Polish law.
A portfolio game is judged in its first 30 seconds: a clean title screen, readable UI, audio feedback, and a clear win/lose loop. A simple game that feels finished beats an ambitious one that feels broken.

Polish checklist

  • Menu scene with title, Play, and Quit.
  • Audio — add AudioSource components; play SFX on pickups/clicks, plus background music.
  • Feedback — particles, screen shake, or a little animation on key events.
  • Restart / win / lose states that always return cleanly to the menu.
  • Icon and product name in Edit > Project Settings > Player.
Lab.
Build a two-scene project: a Menu scene whose Play button loads a Gameplay scene, and a Quit button. Add both to Scenes In Build, then make a WebGL build and open it in a browser.
🎮 Build This: CAPSTONE — Puzzle Game polished to portfolio quality

This STARTS a final new genre (a puzzle game) and is your capstone: take it all the way to a shippable build. You may instead choose to polish any earlier genre — but build something portfolio-ready end to end.

  1. Design a simple grid puzzle (match-3, sliding tiles, or a Sokoban-style box pusher) using prefabs for tiles and 2D input for selection/movement.
  2. Implement the win condition and a move/score counter shown on a Canvas HUD.
  3. Build a Menu scene: title, Play, Quit; and a clean Win screen with Restart and Menu buttons.
  4. Add audio (move/click/win SFX + music), and a little animation or particle feedback on matches/moves.
  5. Add all scenes to Scenes In Build, set product name and icon, and produce a WebGL build.
  6. Upload it to itch.io. You now have a finished, playable, linkable game — across seven genres, all built in Unity. That is your portfolio.
